section_text: The player never spends on punishment despite repeated opportunities and despite being punished themselves, sometimes heavily. This strongly indicates a non-punitive, non-retaliatory style. They neither use punishment to enforce norms nor to get revenge for prior deductions. Their passivity in the punishment stage suggests they may view punishment as inefficient or strategically unprofitable, prioritizing their own earnings over norm enforcement. Alternatively, they may be conflict-averse or indifferent to sanctioning others, even when facing clear under-contributors in the group. Since they are themselves a moderate, sometimes slightly below-average contributor, they might also be reluctant to act as a “moral enforcer” given their own partial free-riding. Overall, they fit a “non-punishing opportunist” profile: they accept the punishment institution as a constraint on their own behavior but do not actively use it to shape others’ actions, and they absorb sanctions without escalating or retaliating.

This player is a selective but recurrent punisher who uses sanctions both normatively and retaliatorily. They are clearly willing to incur personal cost to punish others multiple times across the game, so they are not punishment-averse. The pattern of use suggests that low contributors are salient targets: they appear to employ punishment as a means of enforcing contribution norms and discouraging what they perceive as free-riding. This aligns with a conditional cooperator who wants to sustain a cooperative environment and is willing to “police” it at a cost. At the same time, when they themselves become a target of significant punishment, they do not visibly escalate their own sanctions in a coordinated way, but they do sharply reduce their contributions. This indicates that punishment directed at them is interpreted more as hostility or exploitation than as constructive feedback. Their punishment thus seems more deterrence-oriented when they wield it (aimed at others’ deviations) and more resentment-inducing when they receive it. Because punishers are anonymous, they cannot easily retaliate directly, which may contribute to their withdrawal into low contributions rather than targeted counter-punishment. They show moderate intensity in punishment: they are willing to spend several units in some periods, but they do not engage in extreme, systematic punishment of all below-norm contributors. This suggests a player who values enforcing cooperation but balances that with their own payoff, using punishment as a tool rather than as an overriding goal. Their punishment behavior thus reflects a norm-enforcing, emotionally reactive type who is sensitive to fairness but not committed to maximal sanctioning.

They never use punishment, despite extensive opportunities and despite clear examples of others punishing both defectors and sometimes cooperators. They refrain from punishing chronic low contributors as well as people who punish or talk negatively about the game. This indicates a strong reluctance to impose losses on others, even when doing so might be instrumentally effective or socially endorsed. Their pattern is consistent with an “anti-punisher” or “pure positive enforcement” type: they prefer to shape behavior through rewards, not sanctions. There is no evidence they ever consider punishment as a retaliatory tool, a fairness correction, or a deterrent.

The player is highly reluctant to punish and uses it sparingly and selectively. They employ punishment only once, and only against a clear, repeated free-rider whose contributions are dramatically below group norms, even when most others cooperate. This indicates a threshold-based norm-enforcement motive: they are willing to incur a personal cost to sanction pronounced, persistent non-cooperation but do not punish moderate deviations or late-game partial contributors. After that single act, they do not continue or escalate punishment, even though the target’s behavior gives further justification. This suggests that they may view punishment as a limited corrective signal rather than an ongoing deterrence strategy, or they may be uncomfortable sustaining costly sanctions. They never appear to use punishment retaliatorily; there is no sign they are responding to being targeted, since they themselves are never punished. Overall, they function as a very mild, last-resort enforcer against extreme free-riding, not as a systematic punisher.

They show zero willingness to use punishment, despite having visible examples of others both under- and over-contributing relative to the apparent group norm. This applies equally to clear low contributors and to any perceived norm violators; they do not act against anyone. This strongly suggests an “anti-punishment” or at least “punishment-averse” stance. Likely motives include avoiding costs that don’t have guaranteed personal payoff benefits and possibly a reluctance to engage in confrontation, even when sanctions are transparent and potentially norm-enforcing. There is no evidence that they attempt to use punishment strategically (for deterrence or retaliation), nor that their punishment stance changes with experience; they remain entirely inactive in this channel. Whether this is driven by principle (dislike of harming others) or pure cost–benefit thinking is unknown.

This player makes no use of punishment despite having opportunities and observable cases of lower contributors. They do not engage in either norm-enforcing punishment of low contributors or retaliatory punishment, and they also do not punish apparent “over-spenders” on punishment or reward. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources to reduce others’ payoffs, even when doing so might deter free-riding. Their type aligns with a non-punitive cooperator: they cooperate themselves but do not try to discipline the group. Whether this stems from moral discomfort with harming others, skepticism about punishment effectiveness, or pure payoff-maximizing avoidance of costly sanctioning is unknown. Overall, they prioritize maintaining their own payoff and avoiding conflict over shaping others’ behavior via punishment.

This player is an extremely heavy and emotionally driven punisher, willing to incur very large personal costs to impose large deductions on others. They use punishment both selectively (targeting single individuals) and collectively (hitting all others at once), and their punishment is not consistently aligned with a clear, stable social-efficiency norm. They punish both low contributors and high contributors at different times, so their trigger is not simply “you contributed too little” or “you free-rode on me.” In some instances they heavily punish someone who contributes a moderate or high amount, suggesting that retaliation or frustration plays a role. They also occasionally punish all peers simultaneously regardless of their relative contributions, which looks more like a generalized expression of anger or dominance than measured deterrence. The intensity of punishment is often far beyond what is needed to alter immediate material incentives; they spend enough to drive targets’ round payoffs close to or below zero, and they absorb large self-costs to do so. This pattern is consistent with strong negative reciprocity or spite: they appear willing to “burn money” just to hurt others. There is no clear evidence that they calibrate punishment to marginal changes in others’ contributions; rather, punishment tends to come in large, discrete bursts. They do not appear to adjust punishment downwards in response to others’ subsequent cooperative behavior in any consistent way. After someone they previously punished later contributes highly, they sometimes still punish them again, and in another case massively sanctions a very high contributor. Thus, deterrence or forward-looking incentive design seems secondary at best; expressive or retaliatory motives dominate.

The player never spends any coins on punishment at any point. This holds despite frequent and sometimes extremely heavy use of punishment by others, including punishment targeted at them. Their refusal to punish appears robust to changes in contribution patterns of others, the intensity of others’ punitive behavior, and their own payoff position. They therefore do not act as a norm enforcer, second‑order punisher, or retaliator. This suggests either a strong cost‑aversion to spending on punishment, an instrumental focus on maximizing personal payoffs regardless of others’ behavior, or a belief that their punishment would not be effective or worthwhile. There is no evidence that they use punishment as a deterrent, a signal, or revenge; their stance is non‑punitive and non‑interventionist.

This player is a frequent, sometimes extreme punisher who uses punishment in both targeted and vendetta‑like ways. They clearly accept the cost of punishing as part of their behavioral toolkit and are willing to spend substantial resources to reduce specific others’ payoffs. They do not confine punishment to clear low contributors: they repeatedly punish players who are actually contributing at or near the top of the group distribution. Their punishment pattern toward a small subset of players is persistent and disproportionate to any observable contribution differences. This supports an interpretation of personal grievance or retaliation, rather than normative enforcement of contribution levels. Once a target relationship becomes conflictual, they escalate sanctions rather than de‑escalate, even at high personal cost. This is consistent with a strong taste for reprisal and status assertion: they seem to prefer re‑imposing losses on perceived adversaries over maximizing their own earnings. They also occasionally punish medium or fluctuating contributors, and mix this with reward in other periods, indicating that their criteria are not purely contribution‑based. Their use of punishment does not obviously track efficiency (they sometimes punish in already high‑cooperation environments), suggesting that deterrence of free‑riding is not the primary goal. Instead, their punishment is best described as expressive and relational: they use it to mark disapproval, retaliate against perceived slights, and possibly to test or pressure others, rather than to stabilize group cooperation.

They are an extremely heavy and erratic punisher, willing to spend large amounts on deductions even when everyone is fully cooperating. Punishment is not consistently aligned with contribution behavior: they sometimes hit full cooperators harshly, including those who are positively engaged in chat, and they also punish when there is no contribution deviation at all. Targets shift over time across many different group members, and the intensity varies from light to very heavy, often without any visible behavioral trigger in contributions. They do not use punishment in a straightforward deterrence role (e.g., punishing low contributors to restore norms) and do not follow a clear reciprocity pattern (e.g., focusing on those who previously punished or failed to reward them). In many cases, they spend substantial amounts on punishment in situations where it clearly reduces their own payoff without improving group cooperation (which is already maximal). This points toward a type that is either exploring the punishment space, testing its impact, or acting in a payoff‑insensitive, possibly spiteful or chaotic way rather than enforcing a stable fairness norm. Their willingness to punish is high; their selectivity is low and sometimes appears arbitrary. They also sometimes refrain from any punishment even when deviations occur, showing that they are not mechanically punitive but intermittently aggressive in ways that others cannot easily predict.

They never use punishment, despite being exposed to multiple opportunities where others contribute substantially more or less than they do. This consistent non-use indicates a strong reluctance to spend on sanctioning, even though punishment is relatively efficient in terms of impact per coin spent. Their inaction toward low contributors implies low inclination to enforce norms or deter free-riding at a personal cost. Their inaction toward high contributors implies they are also not using antisocial punishment. The absence of any punishment use makes it impossible to distinguish precisely between “principled non-punisher” and “pure payoff-maximizer,” but it strongly supports that they prioritize conserving their own coins over influencing others’ behavior through costly sanctions.

This player is an “active but selective punisher” who uses costly punishment as a norm-enforcement tool rather than as a constant retaliatory weapon. They do not punish every round and do not systematically target everyone who contributes less; instead, they intervene in specific phases when undercontribution is salient and cooperative norms appear threatened. Their punishment spending is non-trivial at several points, showing a willingness to incur personal costs to sanction others. The likely trigger is visible, repeated low contributions by specific players relative to a generally cooperative group. The size and recurrence of punishment episodes suggest they attempt to correct free-riders or chronic low contributors rather than to fine-tune small deviations. The pattern of when they punish versus when they abstain suggests that their primary motive is deterrence and norm stabilization, not blind revenge. There are instances where they endure substantial punishment from others yet do not immediately respond in kind or escalate punishment expenditure. This indicates that they are not primarily focused on punishing those who harmed them personally, but more on punishing behavior they classify as socially harmful (e.g., zero or very low contributions). They also at times stop punishing after periods of heavier use, even though undercontribution and punishment aimed at them persist. This suggests they learn that further punishment may not be cost-effective or may fuel conflict without improving contributions. They then switch to conserving resources or to signaling via contribution changes instead. Overall, their punishment style is consistent with a “moralist” or “norm enforcer” who is willing to bear costs to discipline defectors early and mid-game, but who becomes more pragmatic and less interventionist when results are discouraging or the cost of mutual sanctioning becomes too high.

They are generally non-punitive and display high tolerance, even when repeatedly targeted by others’ deductions. For almost the entire game they never spend on punishment, despite both clear free-riding by some players and personal unfair treatment. Late in the game they use punishment only once (or very few times; exact identity of punished targets is unknown) when several players are discussing a persistent non-contributor. Given the timing and concurrent chat about particular defectors, this rare use of punishment is likely targeted at clear, repeated free-riders rather than retaliatory or spiteful. Even then, the scale of punishment is low relative to what is possible, indicating reluctance to burn resources on sanctions. Overall they appear punishment-averse and willing to bear costs of exploitation rather than frequently enforce norms, but they are not absolutely opposed to sanctioning in cases of ongoing non-cooperation.

This player is willing to use punishment, but infrequently and in a targeted, norm-enforcing way rather than as retaliation. They never complain in chat about being punished and are never observed responding angrily to deductions, which suggests either that they are not targeted by others or, if they are, they choose not to react publicly. Their own punishment spending is low and appears episodic rather than systematic. Their likely trigger for punishing is clear deviation from an agreed-upon cooperation standard (especially when someone contributes nothing or far less than the group norm) rather than minor shortfalls. They do not appear to punish when contributions are all high or when deviations are plausibly due to confusion, and they do not use punishment to “even the score” or to push others into over-contributing. The lack of repeated or escalating punishment suggests that their primary motive is maintaining a cooperative environment and deterring obvious free-riding, not expressing anger or seeking revenge. Given their overall pattern, they fit the profile of a “light enforcement cooperator”: they mostly rely on mutual understanding and communication to sustain cooperation, resorting to punishment sparingly and only when they judge that group norms are being undermined in a way that threatens the shared outcome. Their willingness to punish at all shows they accept some personal cost to support fairness and discourage exploitation, but the low frequency reflects a preference for harmony and efficiency over aggressive sanctioning.

This player is very willing to use punishment, and uses it in a patterned, targeted way. They consistently direct punishment at the lowest contributors, especially an extreme under-contributor who repeatedly puts in almost nothing despite a cooperative group. They also punish a moderate under-contributor who chronically gives less than the norm. This selectivity implies a norm-enforcing motive rather than random hostility. They rarely, if ever, punish high or average contributors, and they do not appear to retaliate against those who spend heavily on punishment or reward; in fact, they sometimes reward such players. This makes retaliation as a primary motive unlikely. Their willingness to pay large aggregate punishment costs over the game shows that they value norm enforcement and deterrence, even when those costs are substantial relative to their endowment. At one point, they also sanction a player who contributes nothing in a context where almost everyone else contributes very high amounts. This is again consistent with punishing clear norm violators. They later punish a player who makes no contribution at all while most others contribute fully, and again punish the chronic extreme under-contributor even when that player temporarily raises contributions. This indicates that once someone is classified as an untrustworthy cooperator, the player continues to treat them as a target for sanctions, perhaps to reinforce a longer-run reputation or simply to penalize the history of free-riding. They do not appear to use punishment as a fine-tuned marginal adjustment (e.g., scaling punishment to exact contribution gaps); instead, they use a coarse but principled rule: extreme or repeated under-contributors are legitimate targets for strong sanctions. They do not use “anti-social” punishment against strong contributors, and there is no evidence they punish to compete for rank. Their punishment style fits a “strong norm enforcer / altruistic punisher”: willing to incur significant private cost to discipline clear free-riders and hold a cooperation standard.

They never use the punishment mechanism, despite having ample opportunity and a relatively effective punishment technology. This is not due to lack of targets, since there are no defectors; instead, it signals a baseline preference against using costly sanctions when behavior is satisfactory. Because no one misbehaves, their willingness to punish actual free-riding remains unknown. There is no evidence of antisocial or spiteful punishment, nor of using punishment symbolically. They appear content with mutual cooperation and do not seek to enforce norms through threats or deterrence when norms are already followed. Their implicit strategy is “cooperate without sanctioning” in a fully cooperative group, and their punishment preferences in more challenging settings are therefore uncertain (low confidence).

This player never uses the punishment option, even when others free-ride clearly and repeatedly or when some players incur severe punishment from others. Their willingness to punish appears effectively zero under the conditions observed. This suggests either a principled aversion to costly sanctioning, a belief that punishment is inefficient or unnecessary, or indifference to payoff inequality created by free-riding and by others’ sanctioning behavior. They also refrain from “second-order” punishment: they do not punish those who defect, nor those who aggressively punish others. There is no sign of retaliatory motives; when group outcomes worsen because of others’ low contributions, they still do not punish. They behave as a pure contributor who separates cooperation from enforcement, leaving norm maintenance entirely to others if it happens at all. Their use of punishment does not adapt over time because it is consistently unused; this is consistent with a stable “non-punisher” or “laissez-faire cooperator” type.

They never spend any coins on punishment across the game, despite repeated opportunities, visible free‑riding, and social pressure or suggestions to target specific low contributors. Their non‑use of punishment is consistent and appears principled rather than inattentive: they clearly understand the game and actively discuss behaviors, yet decline to impose deductions. They also do not participate in the coordinated, spite‑like punishment campaigns others mount against chronic defectors. This pattern suggests a strong reluctance to pay to harm others, even when punishment is arguably efficiency‑enhancing or norm‑enforcing. Their stance aligns more with a non‑punitive cooperative type than with altruistic punishers or retaliators; they seem unwilling to trade off their own or even others’ payoff efficiency for the sake of sanctioning.

They are a willing and somewhat vindictive punisher. They do not use punishment purely as a calibrated response to low contributions; instead, their targets include at least one fully cooperative, high‑contributor who has publicly complained about being punished. This shows that their punishment is not strictly norm-enforcing or prosocial. They are selective but not careful. They use punishment multiple times, sometimes when group cooperation is high, indicating they are willing to reduce social surplus to pursue personal or emotional goals. Importantly, they explicitly state that being called a bot motivates them to deduct from others. That makes retaliation and perceived disrespect a key trigger. They do not appear to participate in coordinated, group-directed punishment campaigns against obvious free‑riders. Their punishment is idiosyncratic and ego‑driven rather than collectively strategic. At the same time, they do not escalate to extreme, group‑wide punishment; their spending remains moderate compared to the most aggressive punishers.

They never spend any coins on punishment across the game, even when others are persistently free-riding or when they themselves are punished for deviating from the norm. This is consistent over early, middle, and late phases, regardless of how egregious others’ behavior is or how strong the group’s punitive norm becomes. They are thus a pure non-punisher: they forgo both targeted deterrence of free-riders and any retaliatory punishment when they are singled out. Their abstention persists even in clear “mob punishment” situations when punishing a salient low contributor is normatively encouraged in chat and widely practiced by others. This suggests that the absence of punishment is not due to inattention or ignorance of the mechanism; they repeatedly see and live through the costs and effects of punishment but never engage in it. Their priorities appear to favor conserving their own payoff and avoiding active harm to others, even when punishment is socially framed as justified or emotionally satisfying. There is no evidence they use or support punishment as a coordination tool; they seem to treat it as unnecessary or not worth the cost.

They never use the punishment option, even when there are clear low or zero contributors and even when they themselves are punished despite cooperating fully. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on sanctioning, whether for deterrence or retaliation. Their non-use of punishment holds even when social information (chat) highlights specific low contributors, indicating that social cues about norm violations are not sufficient to trigger punitive action. Their motives for avoiding punishment are uncertain: possible explanations include a preference against harming others, a focus on efficiency (not wanting to waste coins), or uncertainty about who is responsible for low cooperation. Because they also refrain from retaliatory punishment when personally targeted, they fit a “non-punishing cooperator” profile with no observed taste for either altruistic or spiteful punishment.

They never use the punishment option at any point, despite having multiple opportunities and clear evidence that others are contributing far more than they are. This suggests low willingness to incur personal cost to enforce norms, either for deterrence or retaliation. Their abstention holds even when someone is clearly targeting them with costly deductions; there is no observable retaliatory behavior. They also do not punish low or declining contributors among others. This combination is consistent with a type that does not invest in second-order enforcement and treats punishment purely as an unnecessary cost. Their motives appear non-vengeful and non-normative: they neither try to discipline free-riders (of which they are one) nor punish those who punish them.

This player is willing to pay nontrivial costs to punish, but their target selection does not align with standard norm-enforcement logic. They impose costly sanctions on high contributors rather than on low contributors, making their punishment pattern appear antisocial or misaligned with typical fairness or reciprocity motives. There is no evidence that they use punishment as a deterrence tool to promote higher cooperation in the group; their punishment is rare, directed at cooperators, and not followed by consistent supportive behavior toward them. Retaliatory motives toward specific individuals cannot be confirmed because they punish players who have not obviously harmed them through low contribution. The player shows no pattern of targeting clear free-riders, suggesting that enforcing contribution norms is not their priority. Overall, their punishment type is best described as “idiosyncratic or possibly spiteful,” with low confidence about the underlying intention.

The player is willing to punish and does so selectively, targeting a clear low contributor rather than anyone who merely contributed less than the average. This indicates a norm-enforcing orientation focused on sanctioning very low or zero contributions rather than fine-grained payoff optimization. They are willing to sacrifice some of their own earnings to impose a substantially larger loss on a perceived free-rider, consistent with a deterrence or justice motive rather than pure spite. They do not escalate or continue punishing once the low-contributing behavior appears to improve; when contributions become uniformly high, they stop punishing, suggesting their use of punishment is contingent on observable norm violations rather than on personal grievances. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment against those who punish them (if any exist), because they do not use punishment later despite themselves being punished; this points to a relatively non-vindictive stance. Their punishment threshold appears high: only clear outliers in under-contribution trigger action.

They are a heavy and frequent user of costly punishment, often spending substantial amounts even when already under intense punitive attack from others. They punish in many rounds, targeting multiple players and sometimes using multiple units per target. Their punishment is not simply directed at low contributors: they sometimes punish very high-contributing players and, conversely, sometimes refrain from punishing low contributors. This indicates their trigger is not just contribution level, but likely perceived hostility, prior sanctions against them, or broader grievances. There is some evidence of retaliation: they punish players who are among the vocal advocates of penalizing low contributors, and they continue to punish despite strong counter-punishment, which points to a willingness to escalate or persist rather than back down. Deterrence appears secondary or ineffective; they do not adjust sanctions to minimize blowback or to efficiently steer others’ contributions. The pattern looks more affective and retributive than strategically calibrated. They also sometimes punish players who are aligned with the majority cooperative norm, which may reflect perceived hypocrisy or personal targeting rather than norm enforcement per se. Overall, they are a norm-conflict or feud-oriented punisher rather than a clean “pro-social punisher.”

The player is clearly willing to use costly punishment and does so selectively against conspicuously low contributors. They target those contributing nothing or very little relative to the group, not medium or high contributors, indicating a fairness-based or norm-enforcing motive rather than spite or rivalry. They punish multiple distinct low contributors across rounds, which suggests a general rule—“severely under-contributing deserves sanctions”—rather than a single-person grudge. Their willingness to pay nontrivial costs to punish, even after being heavily punished themselves by others, implies they treat norm enforcement as valuable enough to trade off against personal earnings. They adjust over time: once certain low contributors express some willingness to improve and the worst defector slightly increases contributions, the player stops punishing and does not escalate, indicating some responsiveness to signals of reform and a bounded, not purely vengeful, enforcement strategy.

This player is a light but nonzero punisher, spending only small amounts on punishment relative to many others. They never engage in extreme sanctioning. When they do punish, it appears targeted: their punishment is rare and low-intensity, not spread broadly. Because individual targets are not visible in the transcript, their exact trigger is unknown, but some patterns are plausible at moderate confidence: - They sometimes punish when there are conspicuous low contributors present, which is consistent with norm-enforcement motives against free-riding. - They never punish in the periods where everyone (or almost everyone) is contributing very highly, even when they themselves are the only low contributor. This suggests their punishment is not retaliatory against those who punish them, nor is it directed at high contributors out of spite. They also punish in contexts where multiple others are already spending heavily on sanctions, suggesting they may be willing to join collective norm enforcement rather than free-ride on others’ punishment. However, their own contributions to punishment remain modest, implying that deterrence is a secondary concern and that they prefer to conserve their own payoff rather than bear large enforcement costs. They never engage in clear counter-punishment of prolific punishers, nor do they appear to punish those who simply mirror the group. Overall, their punishment profile is: selective, low-intensity, likely directed at prominent low contributors, and not driven by revenge.

They are willing to use costly punishment, but only sparingly. When they do punish, it is targeted at clear low contributors rather than at cooperators, consistent with a norm-enforcement motive rather than spite or random aggression. They never engage in visible retaliation against those who might have punished them earlier, and they do not escalate punishment across later rounds even though they continue to be hit by deductions; this supports the view that they do not use punishment for revenge. Their limited and selective use of punishment suggests they weigh its cost and may see heavy punishment as wasteful, preferring to rely mostly on contribution choices and social norms to sustain cooperation.

They never purchase punishment, despite several clear opportunities: repeated under‑contributors, visible heavy punishers, and the fact that they themselves are deducted in some rounds. This indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, regardless of target or context. They do, however, verbally highlight inconsistencies in others’ contributions, which suggests they care about norm violations but prefer non‑monetary signaling over formal punishment. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives: even when they personally lose coins through deductions, they neither escalate via punishment nor change their cooperative behavior. The overall pattern fits a “non‑enforcing cooperator”: willing to sustain the public good and invoke social pressure lightly, but unwilling to incur personal cost to deter or retaliate against free‑riding.

They are generally reluctant to punish and remain inactive in the punishment stage for most of the game, despite having clear opportunities. However, they do engage in costly, targeted punishment of clear free riders, focusing specifically on players who contribute nothing in otherwise cooperative groups. When they do punish, they choose nontrivial but not maximal severity against a repeated free rider, and minimal severity against a one‑off defector, which suggests a graded response: more willing to sanction those who appear persistently noncooperative than those with a single offense. Their punishment seems driven by norm enforcement and fairness concerns rather than retaliation, as they themselves are never targeted for punishment and thus have no reason to strike back. They do not use punishment either to reward high contributors (via “anti-punishment”) or to discipline mild deviations across the board; it is reserved for salient, unambiguous violations. Overall, they are a conditional norm enforcer: mostly tolerant, but willing to incur substantial personal cost to sanction what they likely perceive as repeated or egregious free riding.

They never use punishment, even when some others contribute nothing. They do not respond with sanctions to clear free-riding, despite having both the information and the opportunity. This indicates a strong reluctance to pay personal costs to reduce others’ payoffs. The absence of any selective or minimal punishment suggests they are either punishment-averse in principle, skeptical about the effectiveness of punishment, or strongly focused on preserving their own earnings rather than engaging in norm enforcement through costly sanctions. There is no evidence of deterrence-oriented punishment, expressive punishment, or retaliatory motives. Their “type” is therefore non-punitive: they allow defectors to benefit without direct negative consequences from them.

They are clearly willing to punish, but only in a targeted, rule‑like way. They punish exactly those peers who fail to contribute the maximum when others are contributing fully or nearly fully. They never punish high contributors, and they do not appear to punish for revenge or personal gain, as there is no evidence they ever punish someone who treated them badly in some other way. Punishment is used sparingly, typically as a one‑unit response to a single defection, and not repeated vindictively when the target returns to cooperation. This suggests a norm‑enforcement motive: they use costly punishment to mark under‑contribution as unacceptable and to deter future free‑riding, but they also conserve resources and do not over‑invest in sanctions. Their standard seems simple and strict: “if someone takes the whole endowment while most contribute fully, they deserve a small but clear punishment.”

They punish readily and are willing to incur substantial personal costs to enforce norms. Their punishment is primarily aimed at clear norm violators: almost all punishment targets the chronic non‑contributor, and secondarily those who defect after contributing reliably or who appear to free‑ride while others cooperate. They also on a few occasions punish a cooperator, which looks more like experimentation, misattribution, or noise than a consistent strategy; there is no visible pattern of retaliation for being punished themselves, even when they are heavily deducted in one episode. Their punishment of the chronic non‑contributor is persistent over many rounds, even after that player briefly starts contributing; they still occasionally punish this player thereafter, suggesting a grudging, history‑sensitive stance—past non‑cooperation continues to justify sanctions in their view. They also punish an intermittent non‑contributor and, later, a player who stops contributing while most others continue. They coordinate implicitly with the group’s anti‑free‑rider sentiment and verbally acknowledge ongoing “docking” of the problematic player, confirming that their punishment is motivated by norm enforcement and group solidarity, not by private vendetta. They do not display spiteful punishment of high contributors or anyone who is simply outperforming them. Punishment appears primarily deterrent and expressive of fairness norms, with some willingness to target confusion or perceived insincerity.

They never use punishment, even when a few others contribute noticeably less than the group norm. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on negative sanctions. They neither attempt to correct low contributors nor retaliate for any perceived unfairness. The absence of any punishment across all opportunities indicates that they are either normatively averse to harming others, skeptical about the value of punitive tools, or focused narrowly on conserving resources while cooperation is already high. Their selectivity threshold for punishment is unknown beyond the fact that minor free-riding is tolerated without any visible response. Their punishment motive profile thus matches an “abstainer” type: they rely on contribution choices, not punitive tools, to influence outcomes.

They are generally reluctant to spend resources on punishment and explicitly state that they “don’t want to bother” punishing because it personally costs them coins. Across the game they mostly refrain from punishing, even when others urge group sanctions on defectors. When they do punish, it is selective and targeted: they sanction a clear free‑rider who repeatedly contributes nothing despite group norms, and they also punish a high contributor who fails to talk or appears suspicious/uncooperative in chat. Later they again punish that quiet, intermittently non‑contributing player when their behavior (and slowness) becomes a source of group frustration. This pattern suggests their trigger for punishment is a mix of norm violation (not contributing while others do), perceived untrustworthiness or two‑sided behavior, and process disruption (slowing the game down). Their motive appears more deterrent/norm‑enforcing than vengeful: they combine punishment with open invitations to “join the team” and stop once the defector cooperates consistently. They do not appear to use punishment to settle personal grudges; they engage sporadically when the social or efficiency cost of a player’s behavior feels high enough to overcome their dislike of paying for sanctions.

This player is a frequent and targeted punisher, especially toward clear or repeated non‑contributors and toward those who slow down or disrupt the game. They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to sanction defectors, often choosing multiple targets in a single stage when several fail to contribute. Early on, they punish the initial free‑rider, and later they repeatedly punish the persistent non‑contributor who openly embraces mayhem. They also sanction occasional one‑round noncontributors (such as a player who switches to zero) and later punish an inactive or absent contributor, as well as punishing late‑in‑game non‑contributors. Their pattern shows both norm‑enforcement and some responsiveness to group talk: they echo others’ calls to punish particular defectors and announce their own intention to do so. At times they refrain from punishing even when others call for it, suggesting some selectivity based on their own judgement rather than pure conformity. There are no clear signs of retaliatory punishment toward someone who merely punished them; instead, their targets are consistently associated with low contribution, slow play, or disengagement. They never use punishment frivolously or for jokes; it is always directed at norm violators. They do not escalate to multiple units per person; they appear to treat one unit per offender as an appropriate, cost‑effective sanction.

They are generally reluctant to use punishment and employ it only sparingly and selectively. When they do punish, they target obvious low contributors relative to others, which is consistent with norm-enforcing or second-order free-rider aversion rather than retaliation or spite. They do not escalate punishment or adopt a punitive strategy after being punished themselves; they abstain from punishing in later periods even when they could plausibly retaliate. This pattern indicates low willingness to spend resources on negative sanctions and a preference to avoid interpersonal conflict or costly hostility. Their punishment is best interpreted as occasional, principle-driven norm enforcement with no evidence of sustained deterrence strategy or vendetta behavior. Any belief about the effectiveness of punishment on others’ future contributions is unknown but appears weakly prioritized.

They never use the punishment option at any point. This holds even when there are clear low contributors and active group discussion about whom to punish and how often. They also refrain from participating in attempts to “optimize the pot” via coordinated punishment, despite others explicitly coordinating around this idea. This pattern suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on punishment, regardless of whether the target is a human free‑rider or a suspected bot. Their motives could be: dislike of harming others, skepticism about the instrumental value of punishment, desire to preserve their own payoff, or simple inertia/low engagement; which of these dominates is unknown. They are not observed to punish selectively or reactively; punishment is effectively absent from their strategy. They therefore do not use punishment as a deterrence tool or as a way to enforce fairness norms; instead, they rely solely on their own steadfast cooperation.

They are willing to use punishment, but in a targeted and exploratory way, and they are quite concerned with whether it actually matters. They punish at least two distinct low contributors at different times and escalate once by punishing more heavily, particularly focusing on clearly non-cooperative players. The pattern suggests their triggers are: (1) persistent low or zero contributions and (2) a goal of enforcing norms or testing whether punishment induces behavior change. At the same time, they quickly become skeptical about the effectiveness of punishment, especially when they infer that some targets are bots. They explicitly argue that punishing bots is a waste of resources and urge others not to “waste” their own coins on such actions. Yet their behavior reveals a tension: despite expressing doubt, they still engage in further punishment of low contributors, sometimes framed as a test to “see if it worked.” This indicates a mix of norm-enforcement motives and curiosity-driven experimentation with the mechanism. When others ask for someone to take on punishing duties to preserve the cooperative pot, they volunteer and agree to punish designated targets, signaling willingness to incur costs for the group’s sake. They do not appear to use punishment vindictively; their focus is on non-contributors and bots rather than on interpersonal retaliation. Their underlying type is a “reluctant but cooperative punisher”: they will punish if the group wants it or if non-cooperation is blatant, but they remain wary of wasting resources when deterrence seems unlikely.

They never use the punishment option, even when it is clearly available and widely discussed, and even after they themselves become the primary target of group sanctions. They show no sign of retaliating, disciplining others, or using punishment strategically to shape behavior. This places them as a non‑punisher: they do not incur personal cost to sanction non‑contributors or to defend themselves via counter‑punishment. They also ignore group suggestions that punishment might increase the pot or otherwise be instrumentally beneficial. Whether this reflects a principled aversion to punishment, low understanding of the mechanism, inattention, or simply a pure self‑interest calculation that punishment is not worth the cost is unknown. Their stance is consistent: no punishment is observed at any point, regardless of who is free‑riding or who is punishing them. They are not using punishment as deterrence, norm enforcement, or retaliation.

This player **does punish**, but only in a few select periods and never continuously, indicating that punishment is not their default tool but something they deploy when they feel a specific threshold is crossed. They never punish high contributors; their targets are consistently low contributors or apparent free-riders. This shows a clear norm: non-contribution is viewed as blameworthy, and they are willing to bear a nontrivial personal cost to sanction it. Their punishment is **moderately strong and multi-targeted**: when they use it, they often hit several low contributors at once instead of isolating a single scapegoat. That pattern suggests a primary motive of norm enforcement rather than personal retaliation against one specific player. They also punish some low contributors who have been consistently uncooperative across the game, implying attention to repeated behavior rather than a one-off lapse. At the same time, their punishment pattern is not perfectly consistent. Sometimes they tolerate free-riding without punishing, even when multiple non-contributors stand out. This inconsistency may reflect cost–benefit calculation (punishing only when non-cooperation seems especially widespread), fatigue with paying for sanctions, or uncertainty about how effective punishment really is. There is no clear sign of spite: they do not punish cooperators, they do not over-punish beyond one unit per target, and they do not escalate after being punished themselves. Their behavior fits a **selective, efficiency-minded norm enforcer**: willing to sanction defectors to protect cooperation, but not committed to policing every deviation. They do not appear to use punishment strategically to create fear for future rounds in a systematic way, but they demonstrate a belief that punishing low contributors is legitimate and occasionally necessary.

They never use punishment, even though it is available and relatively effective in terms of impact per unit spent. Because their partner always cooperates, we never observe them in a situation where punishment would be a natural fairness-based response, so their underlying willingness to sanction free-riding is unknown. What we can infer is that they do not use punishment instrumentally (e.g., as a pre-emptive threat or a signal), and they show no inclination to spend resources on norm enforcement when norms are already being followed. Their default stance appears to be non-interventionist: as long as cooperation is high and stable, they refrain from any costly corrective action. Whether this reflects a general aversion to harming others, a desire to maximize their own earnings, or simply the absence of any provocation is uncertain.

The player is a consistent but moderate altruistic punisher. They punish in many rounds but not maximally, and they often choose not to punish in some situations, indicating selectivity. The main trigger is low contribution: they reliably direct punishment toward players who contribute nothing in a given round, especially persistent non-contributors. The intensity of punishment per deviant is limited rather than extreme; they seem to choose levels that impose meaningful deductions while not exhausting their own payoff. There is no clear pattern of revenge aimed at individuals who punished them earlier, and cross-round correlations with deductions they receive are weak or absent, so retaliation motives are either weak or unknown. Their use of punishment across early, middle, and late phases suggests they see it as an ongoing deterrent or sanctioning mechanism, not only a one-off response.

Aside from a single, extremely intense early episode of punishment, this player is effectively non-punitive. Because punishers are anonymous, the exact target profile is unknown, but the context strongly suggests this episode is aimed at low contributors. Overall, they present as a “lapsed or one-shot strong punisher”: initially willing to engage in very costly norm enforcement, then switching to a purely cooperative, non-punitive stance. The absence of later punishment, despite many opportunities, indicates either learning that punishment is inefficient, resignation about changing others’ behavior, or a preference to avoid further conflict; which of these dominates is unknown.

They never use the punishment mechanism, despite frequent and clear opportunities where punitive action would be normatively or strategically justifiable (e.g., persistent low contributors, extremely heavy punishers, or instances where they themselves are punished). The player uses punishment rarely, against cooperative targets, and without any visible justification in the contribution history. Overall, they are a low-frequency, low-intensity, poorly targeted punisher—possibly due to confusion about the mechanism or a weak understanding of its strategic role, rather than deliberate antisocial intent. Confidence in their precise motive for punishment is low.

They are a selective but consistent norm enforcer, targeting one specific player who repeatedly under-contributes. Punishment is directed only at that individual and only when that individual clearly deviates downward from the emerging full-cooperation norm. They do not punish any other deviators (e.g., when another player briefly contributes nothing, or when a different player contributes low once), suggesting their threshold to punish is tied to a pattern of non-compliance by a known norm-breaker more than to any single isolated deviation. Their punishment is not vengeful or escalating; they use a low, fixed intensity that is cost-effective and likely intended as a deterrent and corrective signal rather than as retaliation. Once that target returns to full cooperation for a while, they refrain from further sanctions, but if the same player later lapses again, they resume the same limited punishment. This points to a “conditional enforcer of repeat offenders” style: they punish to sustain cooperation, not to equalize payoffs or to express anger more broadly.

This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment once conflict and opportunism emerge. Early in the game they use punishment sparingly or not at all, despite visible deviations by others, suggesting an initial inclination to give the group a chance to self-stabilize. As the game progresses and some players engage in extreme or persistent punishment campaigns, they shift into an active sanctioning role. Their targets are typically those whose behavior undermines either contribution norms (very low contribution while others are high) or punishment norms (very aggressive or seemingly vindictive use of punishment). They often buy multiple units at once, indicating a willingness to incur substantial personal cost to impose meaningful consequences. Their pattern is more consistent with norm enforcement than random hostility: they do not punish mere small deviations from full contribution when the overall group climate is good, and they refrain from punishing in stable, high-cooperation phases. However, they are not exclusively efficiency-oriented; at times their punishment expenditures are high enough to significantly reduce their own payoffs, indicating that enforcing fairness or expressing disapproval can override narrow self-interest. Overall, they function as a “conditional enforcer”: tolerant when the group is broadly cooperative, but increasingly willing to spend heavily to discipline those they perceive as destabilizing cooperation or abusing the punishment mechanism.

The player supports punishment as a group norm and occasionally participates directly, but is not a heavy or leading punisher. They verbally endorse fining low contributors and agree with others that non-cooperators “will be punished” and “get fined,” indicating approval of sanctioning as a tool for enforcing fairness and group efficiency. Their punishment is selective (aimed at those who under-contribute) and not retaliatory; there is no evidence they punish out of revenge for being sanctioned themselves. The scale of their punishment is modest relative to what other players describe, implying they are willing to incur some cost to uphold norms but are not personally driven to maximize deterrence at all costs. Their stance appears primarily deterrence- and fairness-oriented rather than spiteful: they join the collective effort to discipline defectors but do not escalate or obsess over it.

They never spend any resources on punishment, despite repeated opportunities and clear examples of others using punishment intensively. This is not because there are no tempting targets: there are players who consistently contribute less than most others and are already being sanctioned by others. The player nonetheless abstains. Their type thus aligns with a non-punishing, non-retaliatory strategy, focused on adjusting contribution levels rather than using formal sanctions to shape others.

The player never uses punishment, even when others contribute markedly less than them and even when those low contributors impose large external costs on the group via their behavior or are themselves heavily punished by others. This consistent non-use suggests either a strong aversion to harming others, a focus on personal payoff preservation, or both, but their persistent full cooperation indicates that material self-interest alone is unlikely to be the driving factor. They show no sign of “moralistic” or “norm-enforcing” punishment, even when free-riding is clear and when the punishment technology is highly effective. There is no indication of selective or targeted punishment; the player simply opts out of this channel entirely. Their motives for non-punishment—whether principled pacifism, discomfort with punitive actions, or a belief that punishment is socially or materially counterproductive—are unknown from the data, but the behavioral pattern is strong: they are a non-punisher by type in this setting.

They very rarely punish and, when they do, they spend only minimal amounts. Their punishment is sparse across the game and does not escalate in response to others’ behavior. Given the environment, where other players frequently levy large and often extreme punishments (including what looks like antisocial or chaotic punishment), this player’s restraint stands out: they almost always abstain, even when clear defectors or highly disruptive punishers are present. This suggests a strong preference for conserving own payoff and avoiding engagement in punishment battles, or a belief that punishment is either ineffective, too costly, or not their role. When they do punish, the timing aligns with situations where some others contribute less, but given the very small magnitude, it is unclear whether they are attempting norm enforcement or simply experimenting with the mechanism; confidence on their motives is low. They do not engage in retaliatory or spiteful punishment cycles, and they make no visible systematic attempt to deter specific low contributors or pathological punishers.

The player never uses punishment at any point, despite frequent and often extreme punishment activity by others and clear opportunities to target both low contributors and those who punish aggressively. This strongly suggests a “non-punisher” or “punishment-averse cooperator” type. Several interpretations are possible: they may dislike inflicting costs on others even when it could deter free‑riding; they may view punishment as wasteful and efficiency-reducing; or they may be conflict-averse and unwilling to single others out. Their strategy therefore uses no punitive leverage at all, even though the mechanism is powerful and cheap in this environment.

Punishment is central to this player’s type. They are a frequent and often heavy user of punishment, at times spending a large share of their payoff to reduce others’ payoffs. Their punishment is not random: it is closely tied to others’ contribution behavior and appears to follow a relatively coherent logic, though it is applied more aggressively than is payoff-maximizing. They most plausibly target very low contributors or clear free riders, especially when the public good level is otherwise moderate or high. This implies a strong aversion to perceived exploitation: they are willing to sacrifice substantial coins to discipline those they see as not doing their fair share. Their punishment is therefore primarily norm-enforcing rather than spiteful or arbitrary. When others’ contributions are broadly high, they are still willing to punish isolated low or zero contributors rather than ignoring them. They also continue to punish even after being punished themselves, and after observing that their own payoff can be severely reduced by others’ punitive actions. This persistence indicates that their punishment is not purely reciprocal retaliation but is driven by a robust commitment to enforcing contribution norms, even under the threat of counter-punishment. However, there is at least some retaliatory element: after receiving significant punishment while behaving cooperatively, they do not abandon the use of punishment, suggesting they may respond to perceived injustice with further punitive action. Their use of punishment is not marginal or purely symbolic; they often spend enough to substantially depress their own payoff, indicating either (a) strong negative emotional response to free riding, (b) a belief that harsh sanctions are necessary to change behavior, or (c) both. They do not appear to carefully calibrate punishment to cost-effectiveness; instead they err on the side of intensity, signaling a “hardline enforcer” personality—norms and fairness are protected even at high personal cost. In summary, this player is a conditional cooperator and strong norm enforcer, with high tolerance for costly punishment and a willingness to trade off immediate material gain in order to punish perceived unfairness and attempt to steer group behavior.

This player is a strong, targeted punisher who focuses primarily on the most extreme free-rider. They are willing to pay substantial personal costs to sanction someone who repeatedly contributes almost nothing, far beyond what would be payoff-maximizing in a narrow economic sense. They also do not systematically punish everyone who ever under-contributes; instead, they concentrate on a salient low contributor and, later, on another player who contributes very little relative to the group and to this player’s own contribution. They never use reward, even when others contribute highly, suggesting a preference for “stick” over “carrot” as a regulatory tool. Their willingness to escalate punishment against a persistent low contributor is very high and includes extremely costly sanctioning, pointing to a personality profile consistent with strong negative reciprocity and possible “altruistic punishment”: they accept large personal losses to uphold their cooperation norm and to penalize norm violators.

They are extremely reluctant to punish and almost never use the punishment mechanism, even when they themselves are repeatedly targeted. For most of the game, they spend nothing on punishment, despite substantial and clearly observable deductions aimed at them when they deviate from contribution norms. They are a frequent and costly punisher. The willingness to keep paying to punish late in the game, when learning benefits are limited, suggests a strong intrinsic preference for fairness and norm compliance over purely instrumental punishment.

They never use punishment against any peer, even when others clearly contribute less or when they themselves are monetarily harmed via others’ use of the mechanism. This is consistent with a prosocial, non-confrontational type who prefers positive reinforcement and cooperative norms over deterrence through sanctions.

This player is an active, norm-enforcing punisher. They use punishment in most rounds and are willing to incur non-trivial personal costs to do so, indicating that maintaining contribution norms matters to them more than maximizing immediate earnings. Their pattern is consistent with “altruistic punishment”: they invest resources to sanction behavior that appears uncooperative, with no direct material reward. They seem primarily triggered by noticeably low contributions rather than minor deviations. Players who repeatedly or dramatically under-contribute relative to the apparent cooperative cluster are likely targets. The persistence of punishment costs suggests they view it as a tool for deterrence and norm stabilization rather than a one-off expression of frustration. There is no clear evidence that they punish high contributors, so “antisocial punishment” cannot be inferred and is more likely absent, but this is low confidence. Their willingness to invest multiple units in punishment across several rounds, even when it reduces their own payoff substantially, signals strong commitment to sanctioning perceived free-riding. At the same time, they do pause punishment in at least one instance, suggesting they are not mechanically punishing every time, and may be responsive to temporary improvements or uncertainty about others’ intentions.

They never use the punishment option, despite clear knowledge of its availability and a group context where some players occasionally defect. The player never spends any resources on punishment, even when others clearly defect or when targetable free-riding or confusing behavior occurs. They are generally reluctant to use punishment and do so only in a very focused, norm‑enforcing way. They never use the punishment option, despite repeated opportunities and despite clear deviations by several others from cooperative norms. This player never spends on punishment, despite repeated opportunities and clear cases of free-riding by others. They also never retaliate after being punished themselves. This player is a selective but willing punisher of clear free-riding. The player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment. They are willing to incur nontrivial personal costs to reduce others’ payoffs, and they do so in multiple rounds. They never use punishment at any point, even when they are personally targeted by punishment and even when some players free‑ride strongly or behave erratically. This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment. They are clearly willing to spend nontrivial amounts of their own payoff to impose costs on others. They often punish even when they are themselves contributing very little, which suggests that their punishment is not purely altruistic or tied to their own personal cooperative sacrifice. This raises two compatible interpretations: (1) they may hold a view that certain minimal levels of cooperation are required from everyone, regardless of their own exact contribution in that round (a “you must at least try” standard), or (2) they may use punishment in a more strategic or emotionally driven way, enforcing norms selectively while continuing to free-ride themselves. Their punishment is sometimes heavy relative to their round payoff, indicating they are willing to accept sizable immediate personal costs in order to deter or sanction others. This behavior is consistent with a player who values shaping the group’s behavior or expressing disapproval more than maximizing immediate earnings. The pattern of repeated punishment toward the end of the game, even when they themselves are also being punished, suggests that once conflicts are established they tend to escalate rather than de-escalate. They do not always punish when low contributions by others are present, indicating some selectivity. This could reflect budget management, shifting focus to those seen as persistent offenders, or simple experimentation with when punishment is most effective. Because reward is unavailable, their only direct instrument for influencing others is negative; they appear willing to use it regularly and sometimes aggressively. In summary, their punishment profile fits a “selectively harsh enforcer with self-interest” type: they enforce contribution norms, likely focusing on low contributors, but do so even when their own cooperative stance is ambiguous, and they are prepared to bear significant costs to punish.

This player is a pure non-punisher in this setting, even when they themselves deviate or are sanctioned. Overall, they are punishment-averse and non-confrontational.

This player never uses costly punishment, even in the presence of clear free-riders and even after being punished themselves. This suggests a strong reluctance to engage in negative sanctioning and possibly an aversion to harming others, including those who behave selfishly.
